skill.name: combat-ui-pattern-a
skill.description: Build the SHINOBI WAY split-panel combat interface with React, TypeScript, and Tailwind CSS. The skill guides component structure, props, layout, styling tokens, animations, and migration from the existing combat scene.
skill.summary: Create a split-panel combat UI system for a React game.
skill.author: CesarAugustusGroB

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are almost entirely Markdown and TypeScript UI examples. The only real external command guidance is an optional scaffolding block with mkdir and touch, so the skill is low risk and safe to publish with awareness of workspace file creation.

low_findings.title: Benign Scaffolding Shell Commands
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for command presence, but low risk. The Bash block creates a combat component directory and empty React component files; it does not download code, access secrets, execute dynamic input, or remove files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in a Bash documentation block and are limited to mkdir and touch. They modify the workspace only when a user chooses to run the optional scaffolding step.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Execution False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for the repeated Ruby or shell backtick detections. The flagged files use Markdown code fences, inline code formatting, and TypeScript template literals for UI examples; no Ruby backtick execution or shell command substitution was found in those references.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence consists of Markdown and TypeScript examples, not executable Ruby or shell syntax. Broad review found no dynamic command execution path.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for weak cryptographic algorithm alerts. The cited contexts contain Tailwind breakpoint prefixes such as md and lg, TypeScript action type filtering, prose, or component comments; no hashing or cryptographic API usage was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged terms are UI breakpoint names and TypeScript sample code. Targeted review found no MD5, SHA-1, crypto library calls, or password hashing guidance.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for system reconnaissance alerts. The cited material describes CSS grid structure, responsive screen layout, and animation state; no host discovery commands or environment inspection instructions were found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious words appear in UI layout and animation contexts, such as screen sizing and floating text IDs. No commands like whoami, uname, network inspection, or file enumeration were present.

content.value_statement: Combat screens need clear structure for turn state, character status, and actions. This skill provides a React and Tailwind blueprint for a split-panel battle interface.
actual_capabilities: Defines the component hierarchy for a split-panel combat screen.
Provides TypeScript prop interfaces for combat UI components.
Specifies Tailwind layout patterns for desktop and mobile views.
Documents styling tokens for panels, cards, bars, buttons, and text.
Describes animation hooks and classes for combat feedback.
Outlines a staged migration from the existing combat scene.
limitations: It is tailored to the SHINOBI WAY combat architecture.
It does not include a complete runnable application.
It assumes React, TypeScript, and Tailwind CSS are available.
It may need adaptation for different state models or game rules.

use_cases.title: Refactor a Combat Scene
use_cases.description: Convert an existing vertical combat view into a split-panel player versus enemy layout.

use_cases.title: Implement Reusable Battle Components
use_cases.description: Create typed React components for character panels, action cards, phase headers, and the VS divider.

use_cases.title: Standardize Game UI Styling
use_cases.description: Apply shared Tailwind tokens for combat panels, resource bars, skill cards, and responsive behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Start the Layout
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for the main combat screen.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the combat-ui-pattern-a skill to create the CombatLayout component with the documented split-panel grid and mobile fallback.

prompt_templates.title: Build Character Panels
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the component interfaces and styling tokens to implement CharacterPanel for player and enemy variants.

prompt_templates.title: Create the Action Dock
prompt_templates.scenario: Implement turn actions and skill cards.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ActionDock, QuickActionCard, and MainActionCard references to build the bottom combat action area.

prompt_templates.title: Migrate the Full Combat View
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced refactor of an existing scene.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the migration strategy to replace the old combat scene with Pattern A components while preserving callbacks and combat state.

output_examples.input: Create the PhaseHeader for the combat screen.
output_examples.output: A typed React component plan with turn indicator, phase pipeline, side action counter, and approach modifier sections.
A layout approach that stacks panels on small screens and switches to a three-column confrontation view on tablet and desktop sizes.
Animation guidance for floating text, screen shake, character attacks, resource bars, and reduced motion support.

best_practices: Start with CombatLayout before extracting smaller components.
Keep props aligned with the documented interfaces and existing combat state.
Preserve accessibility labels and reduced motion support during implementation.
anti_patterns: Do not rewrite combat rules while changing the visual layout.
Do not hardcode skill data inside presentation components.
Do not remove mobile fallback behavior from the split-panel layout.

faq.question: What framework does this skill target?
faq.answer: It targets React with TypeScript and Tailwind CSS.

faq.question: Can it build a complete game combat system?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on UI components and uses existing combat state and callbacks.

faq.question: Does it include responsive design guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents mobile stacking and tablet or desktop split-panel layouts.

faq.question: Does it define component props?
faq.answer: Yes. The references include interfaces for layout, panels, action cards, headers, and floating text.

faq.question: Does it require running shell commands?
faq.answer: No. It includes optional scaffolding commands that create directories and empty component files.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
